lessons.title: Acids and Bases — Properties and pH

content.body: Acids and bases are two of the most important classes of chemical compounds. They are found everywhere — in food, medicines, cleaning products, and our own bodies.

Arrhenius definitions:
Acid: produces H⁺ ions in water. e.g. HCl → H⁺ + Cl⁻
Base: produces OH⁻ ions in water. e.g. NaOH → Na⁺ + OH⁻

Brønsted-Lowry:
Acid: a proton (H⁺) donor
Base: a proton (H⁺) acceptor

pH Scale (0–14):
pH 0–6: Acidic (lower = more acidic)
pH 7: Neutral (pure water)
pH 8–14: Alkaline/Basic (higher = more alkaline)

Strong acids: HCl (hydrochloric), H₂SO₄ (sulphuric), HNO₃ (nitric)
Strong bases: NaOH (sodium hydroxide), KOH (potassium hydroxide)

content.title: Common Acids and Bases in Daily Life
content.body:
Acids:
• Lemon juice / vinegar: pH 2-3 (citric acid, acetic acid)
• Stomach acid: pH 1-2 (hydrochloric acid)
• Fizzy drinks: pH 3-4 (carbonic acid)
• Rain water: pH ~5.6 (carbonic acid from CO₂)

Bases:
• Baking soda: pH ~8 (sodium bicarbonate)
• Soap: pH 9-10
• Ammonia cleaning solution: pH 11
• Bleach: pH 12

Neutralisation reaction:
Acid + Base → Salt + Water
HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O
H₂SO₄ + 2KOH → K₂SO₄ + 2H₂O

Indicators tell us if a solution is acidic/neutral/basic:
Litmus: red in acid, blue in alkali
Universal indicator: full range of colours
phenolphthalein: colourless in acid, pink in alkali

questions.question: What pH is neutral?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral — pure water has pH 7.

questions.question: An acid produces which ion in water?
questions.explanation: Acids donate protons (H⁺ ions) in water.

questions.question: What is the product of acid + base reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water.

questions.question: Stomach acid has pH of about 1-2. This means it is:
questions.explanation: pH 1-2 is at the very acidic end of the pH scale.

questions.question: Litmus paper turns RED in:
questions.explanation: Red litmus stays red in acid; blue litmus turns red in acid. Litmus turns blue in alkali.

questions.question: Which of these is a strong base?
questions.explanation: Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) is a strong base used in drain cleaners, soap making, and paper production.

questions.question: HCl + NaOH → ?
questions.explanation: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O (salt sodium chloride + water).

questions.question: Universal indicator is used to:
options: Give the exact pH of a solution
Show if a reaction is complete
questions.answer: Give the exact pH of a solution
questions.explanation: Universal indicator changes through a range of colours corresponding to different pH values.

questions.question: Acid rain has a pH of about 4. It is caused by:
options: SO₂ and NOₓ from burning fossil fuels
questions.answer: SO₂ and NOₓ from burning fossil fuels
questions.explanation: SO₂ and NOₓ from industrial/vehicle emissions dissolve in rainwater to form sulphuric and nitric acids.

questions.question: A Brønsted-Lowry base is:
questions.explanation: Brønsted-Lowry: acid = proton (H⁺) donor; base = proton (H⁺) acceptor.

content.body: The Periodic Table is the most important tool in chemistry. It organises all 118 known elements by atomic number, and reveals patterns in their properties. It was developed by Dmitri Mendeleev in 1869.

Organisation of the Periodic Table:
Periods (rows): elements in the same row have the same number of electron shells
Groups (columns): elements in the same column have the same number of outer electrons → similar chemical properties

Group 1: Alkali Metals (Li, Na, K, Rb, Cs, Fr)
Group 7: Halogens (F, Cl, Br, I, At)
Group 0/18: Noble Gases (He, Ne, Ar, Kr, Xe, Rn)
Middle section: Transition Metals (Fe, Cu, Zn, etc.)

content.title: Group 1 — Alkali Metals
content.body:
Properties of Group 1 metals:
• Soft metals that can be cut with a knife
• React vigorously with water, producing hydrogen gas and an alkali
• 2Na + 2H₂O → 2NaOH + H₂
• Reactivity INCREASES going DOWN the group (outer electron farther from nucleus, easier to lose)
• Stored in oil to prevent reaction with air/moisture

content.title: Group 7 — Halogens
content.body:
Properties of Group 7 (non-metals):
• F₂: pale yellow gas (most reactive)
• Cl₂: green-yellow gas
• Br₂: red-brown liquid
• I₂: grey-black solid (purple vapour)
• Reactivity DECREASES going DOWN the group
• Form salts with metals: 2Na + Cl₂ → 2NaCl
• More reactive halogen displaces less reactive: Cl₂ + 2KBr → 2KCl + Br₂

Group 0 (Noble Gases): He, Ne, Ar, Kr, Xe, Rn.
They are extremely unreactive (inert) because they have full outer electron shells.
He is used in balloons. Ar is used in light bulbs. Ne is used in neon signs.

questions.question: Who developed the Periodic Table?
questions.explanation: Dmitri Mendeleev arranged elements by atomic mass and predicted properties of undiscovered elements in 1869.

questions.question: Elements in the same GROUP have:
questions.explanation: Elements in the same group have the same number of electrons in their outer shell, giving similar chemical properties.

questions.question: Which group are the alkali metals?
questions.explanation: Group 1 contains the alkali metals: Li, Na, K, Rb, Cs, Fr.

questions.question: Going DOWN Group 1, reactivity:
questions.explanation: Going down Group 1, the outer electron is farther from the nucleus and easier to lose → more reactive.

questions.question: Why are noble gases unreactive?
options: They have a full outer electron shell
questions.answer: They have a full outer electron shell
questions.explanation: Noble gases have 8 outer electrons (He has 2) — a full stable shell means no tendency to react.

questions.question: What is produced when sodium reacts with water?
questions.explanation: 2Na + 2H₂O → 2NaOH + H₂↑. Sodium hydroxide (alkali) and hydrogen gas are produced.

questions.question: Going DOWN Group 7, reactivity:
questions.explanation: Going down Group 7, halogens become less reactive. Fluorine is most reactive, iodine least.

questions.question: Chlorine reacts with potassium bromide solution. What happens?
options: Nothing — Cl₂ is less reactive than Br₂
questions.explanation: More reactive halogens displace less reactive ones. Cl₂ is above Br₂ in reactivity, so Cl₂ displaces Br₂.

questions.question: What is the state of bromine at room temperature?
questions.explanation: Bromine (Br₂) is a red-brown liquid at room temperature — it is the only non-metal liquid element.

questions.question: Argon gas is used in light bulbs because:
options: It is cheap and inert — won't react with the filament
questions.answer: It is cheap and inert — won't react with the filament
questions.explanation: Argon is a noble gas — completely unreactive. This prevents the tungsten filament from burning.
